title: Cigarette Smoke Extract Alters the Inflammatory Secretome of Osteoarthritis-Affected Synovium Increasing Joint Tissue Oxidative Stress in an Indirect Co-Culture Model

sections.title: 1 Introduction
sections.content: Osteoarthritis (OA) is the leading cause of adult chronic pain and long-term disability worldwide [ 1 , 2 ]. In 2020, there were more than 650 million individuals globally aged 40 and older with knee OA [ 3 ]. Knee OA is a whole joint disease that is characterized by biomechanical and biochemical changes to all joint tissues, including cartilage, meniscus, ligaments, tendons, synovium, subchondral bone, the infrapatellar fat pad (IFP) [ 4 ]. Disease progression is mediated by chronic inflammation, where elevated levels of proinflammatory cytokines interleukin-1 beta (IL-1β) and tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α) promote the up-regulation of matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) causing a loss of glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) and destruction of articular cartilage [ 5 , 6 ]. Additionally, OA synovial fibroblasts and macrophages can secrete TNF-α and IL-1β to further induce cartilage loss [ 4 , 7 - 9 ]. More specifically, macrophages can be stimulated to polarize to different phenotypes, including the pro-inflammatory M1 phenotype and the anti-inflammatory/tissue-repairing M2 phenotype, where imbalanced ratios of M1/M2 macrophages have been associated with OA severity [ 10 ]. It has also been suggested that there is possible crosstalk between the synovial membrane and IFP, which secretes its own proinflammatory cytokines and adipokines that may contribute to disease progression [ 11 , 12 ]. Combined, this ultimately leads to a dysregulation in the homeostasis of anabolic and catabolic processes in the joint, resulting in joint tissue destruction, pain, and disability. Despite the current understanding of how risk factors such as age, obesity, and previous joint injury contribute to OA, the field continues identifying new mechanisms underpinning OA progression, for example, the influence of various metabolic abnormalities and proteomic profiles [ 13 , 14 ]. One underexplored risk factor is cigarette smoking. Currently, smoking accounts for the most significant cause of preventable deaths in the world, where, in 2021, cigarette smoking was responsible for more deaths than COVID-19 [ 15 , 16 ]. Further, in a study by the World Health Organization (WHO), the prevalence in combustible cigarette use was predicted to increase in males until the year 2040, demonstrating that cigarette smoking is still a relevant problem across the globe [ 17 , 18 ]. Although the effects of smoking on OA have been inconsistent for decades, increasing evidence suggests that it may accelerate cartilage damage, but the mechanisms behind this need to be explored further [ 19 - 22 ]. This is important as smoking has been shown to impact the therapeutic efficacy of anti-TNF treatments in patients with rheumatoid arthritis (RA) [ 23 ]. Previous work by our lab has shown that in a cartilage explant-only model, cigarette smoke extract (CSE) combined with OA-inducing cytokines IL-1β and TNF-α exacerbates cartilage explant degradation, resulting in greater losses in viability, more sGAG loss, and more ROS production than OA pro-inflammatory cytokines alone [ 24 ]. However, investigations on the direct effects of CSE on OA-affected synovium and the synovial secretome have yet to be undertaken. Water-soluble oxidants from cigarette smoke have been shown to enter systemic circulation and other body fluids, leading to sustained oxidative damage in different organ systems and elevating inflammatory markers in serum, blood, and vascular tissue [ 25 - 27 ]. Further, oxidative damage to the synovium has been shown to exacerbate synovial inflammation and contribute to downstream cartilage loss [ 28 , 29 ]. As such, the major aims of this study were to 1) determine the effects that CSE has on synovial oxidative stress and inflammation, and 2) the resulting impact these changes have on cartilage loss. Based on the systemic damage cigarette smoking has on the body, we hypothesized that synovium cultured in CSE + OA-like inflammation would have reduced viability and metabolic activity, increased ROS, and a shift in the ratio of M1 to M2 macrophages and, therefore, will produce more inflammatory cytokines and MMPs compared to synovium exposed to OA-like inflammation only. In response, cartilage explants cultured in CSE + OA synovium conditioned media (SCM) would experience greater losses of viability, metabolic activity, GAGs, and ECM integrity than cartilage explants exposed to OA-like inflammation only SCM.

subsections.title: 2.1 Reagents and Materials
subsections.content: Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM; Gibco, Thermo Fisher Scientific), insulin-transferrin-selenium (ITS; Gibco), fetal bovine serum (FBS; Gibco), antibiotic/antimycotic (Ab/Am; Gibco), Normocin (InvivoGen), ascorbate-2-phosphate (Sigma-Aldrich), and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS; Gibco) were used for all explant culture and acclimation procedures. Cigarette smoke extract was generated using 1R6F research cigarettes (Center for Tobacco Reference Products, University of Kentucky) and sterile filtered through 0.22 μm filters (MilliporeSigma). Recombinant porcine IL-1β and TNF-α were obtained from R&D Systems. Live/Dead viability staining utilized Calcein-AM (Thermo Fisher Scientific) and Ethidium Homodimer-III (Biotium). Metabolic activity was assessed using AlamarBlue reagent (Invitrogen). Papain digestion reagent, dimethyl methylene blue dye, and chondroitin sulfate standards were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. DNA content was quantified using the Quant-iT PicoGreen dsDNA assay kit (Invitrogen). For histological analyses, 10% neutral buffered formalin (Fisher Scientific), Safranin-O/Fast Green staining reagents (Electron Microscopy Sciences), and hematoxylin (Vector Laboratories) were used. Immunohistochemistry was performed using primary antibodies against CD86, CD163, and CD68 (Abcam), and the ImPRESS Excel Amplified Polymer Detection Kit (Vector Laboratories). Mitochondrial superoxide was detected using MitoSOX Red reagent (Invitrogen). Cytokine and protease profiling of synovial conditioned medium was performed using the Human Inflammation Array GS1 (RayBiotech, GSH-INF-1) and Human MMP Array Q1 (RayBiotech, QAH-MMP-1).

subsections.title: 2.2 Generation of Cigarette Smoke Extract (CSE)
subsections.content: 100% CSE was freshly prepared every 3 days by smoking one 1R6F research cigarette directly into 22.5 mL DMEM utilizing a syringe-based smoking apparatus, as previously described [ 24 ]. Each cigarette was smoked following the guidelines in ISO 3308. 100% CSE was sterile filtered through a 0.22 μm filter to remove undissolved particulate. 100% CSE was then normalized to its optical density (OD) at a wavelength of 320 nm [ 22 ]. Acceptable OD values for 100% CSE batches were 0.70 ± 0.06. 100% CSE was diluted down to 10% CSE for culture, corresponding to heavy smoking habits (27 cigarettes/day), and pH was adjusted to 7.4 [ 24 ].
paragraphs: 100% CSE was freshly prepared every 3 days by smoking one 1R6F research cigarette directly into 22.5 mL DMEM utilizing a syringe-based smoking apparatus, as previously described [ 24 ]. Each cigarette was smoked following the guidelines in ISO 3308. 100% CSE was sterile filtered through a 0.22 μm filter to remove undissolved particulate. 100% CSE was then normalized to its optical density (OD) at a wavelength of 320 nm [ 22 ]. Acceptable OD values for 100% CSE batches were 0.70 ± 0.06. 100% CSE was diluted down to 10% CSE for culture, corresponding to heavy smoking habits (27 cigarettes/day), and pH was adjusted to 7.4 [ 24 ].

subsections.title: 2.3 Isolation and Acclimation of Synovium and Cartilage Explants
subsections.content: Tibiofemoral joints from 6- to 8-month-old pigs (male and female, n = 6) were obtained from a commercial abattoir as a byproduct of the agricultural industry (Snow Creek Meat Processing, Seneca, SC). Under aseptic conditions, synovium with the joint capsule from the weight-bearing region of the joint was excised and placed directly into sterile phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) with 2% Ab/Am. The tissue was divided into approximately 5 mm x 5 mm pieces and returned to fresh PBS + 2% Ab/Am. Cartilage explants were isolated from the weight-bearing, distal femoral condyles using a 5 mm biopsy punch. Synovium and cartilage were individually placed into the wells of non-treated 12-well plates and incubated in acclimation media containing DMEM, 1x ITS, 2% FBS, 2% Ab/AM, 100 µg/mL Normocin, and 50 nM ascorbate-2-phosphate at 37°C with 5% CO 2 for 3 days.
paragraphs: Tibiofemoral joints from 6- to 8-month-old pigs (male and female, n = 6) were obtained from a commercial abattoir as a byproduct of the agricultural industry (Snow Creek Meat Processing, Seneca, SC). Under aseptic conditions, synovium with the joint capsule from the weight-bearing region of the joint was excised and placed directly into sterile phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) with 2% Ab/Am. The tissue was divided into approximately 5 mm x 5 mm pieces and returned to fresh PBS + 2% Ab/Am. Cartilage explants were isolated from the weight-bearing, distal femoral condyles using a 5 mm biopsy punch. Synovium and cartilage were individually placed into the wells of non-treated 12-well plates and incubated in acclimation media containing DMEM, 1x ITS, 2% FBS, 2% Ab/AM, 100 µg/mL Normocin, and 50 nM ascorbate-2-phosphate at 37°C with 5% CO 2 for 3 days.

subsections.title: 2.4 Conditioned Medium Preparation and Indirect Co-Culture of Synovium and Cartilage Explants
subsections.content: An indirect co-culture model was used to investigate the effects of CSE on OA-like inflammation in synovium and the resulting consequences on cartilage explant degradation. After acclimation, synovium was randomly divided into three groups: controls ( n = 8), OA only (explant media supplemented with 10 ng/mL IL-1β and 10 ng/mL TNF-α; n = 8), and CSE + OA (explant media supplemented with 10 ng/mL IL-1β and 10 ng/mL TNF-α and 10% CSE; n = 8) and received 2 mL of fresh, supplemented media every 3 days. After 3, 6, and 9 days of treatment, synovium conditioned media (SCM) were used to treat cartilage explants, and AlamarBlue assay was performed on synovium. On Day 9, synovium was divided into 3 sections for viability, ROS, and histological analysis as previously described [ 24 ]. After their initial acclimation, cartilage explants were divided into the same groups as the synovium: controls ( n = 8), OA only ( n = 8), and CSE + OA ( n = 8), and acclimated for three more days whereby controls received control medium, and OA only and CSE + OA cartilage explants received OA only media. SCM from days 3, 6, and 9 of synovium culture was transferred directly from the synovium to a paired cartilage explant (Supporting Figure 1A,B ). Then, after 3, 6, and 9 days of cartilage exposure to SCM, cartilage media was aliquoted and saved, and AlamarBlue assay was performed on cartilage explants. On Day 9 of treatment with SCM, cartilage explants were divided for viability, ROS, biochemical and histological analyses as previously described [ 24 ].

subsections.title: 2.5 Assessing Tissue Viability and Metabolic Activity
subsections.content: After 9 days, synovium explant viability ( n = 4/group/time point) was assessed via Live/Dead assay by incubating explants with Calcein-AM (10 µM) and Ethidium Homodimer-III (3 µM) according to the manufacturer's instructions. Fluorescent imaging was completed using a Keyence BZX 800 (20x total magnification). After 9 days, cartilage explant viability ( n = 4/group/time point) was also evaluated via Live/Dead assay as previously described [ 24 ]. Fluorescent imaging was completed using a Keyence BZX 800 (400x total mag.). The number of living and dead cells per high power field (HPF) was counted using CellProfiler software. Percent viability was calculated by dividing the number of live cells by the total number of living and dead cells multiplied by 100. On days 3, 6, and 9, AlamarBlue assay was performed on synovium and cartilage explants. AlamarBlue reagent was diluted to 10% with control medium, and all explants were incubated for 4 h at 37°C and 5% CO 2 . Media was removed from each explant, pipetted in duplicates into 96-well plates, and read at 530 excitation/590 emission (Agilent BioTek Synergy H1 plate reader).

subsections.title: 2.6 Dimethyl Methylene Blue Assay for Glycosaminoglycan Content Analysis
subsections.content: For changes in sulfated GAG (sGAG) content, cartilage explants were frozen at −80°C overnight, lyophilized for 48 h, and dry weights were recorded. Explants were digested with papain overnight at 65°C. DMMB assay was then performed on all digested explants, utilizing a standard curve made from chondroitin-sulfate to quantify sGAGs in each tissue (Agilent BioTek Synergy H1 plate reader). For sGAG loss, DMMB assay was performed on explant media collected on day 3, 6, and 9, and percent sGAG loss was calculated as previously described [ 24 ].

Explants were digested with papain overnight at 65°C. DMMB assay was then performed on all digested explants, utilizing a standard curve made from chondroitin-sulfate to quantify sGAGs in each tissue (Agilent BioTek Synergy H1 plate reader). For sGAG loss, DMMB assay was performed on explant media collected on day 3, 6, and 9, and percent sGAG loss was calculated as previously described [ 24 ].

subsections.title: 2.7 DNA Content of Synovium and Cartilage Explants
subsections.content: Synovium and cartilage were enzymatically digested with papain at 65°C overnight. Tissue digestions were frozen overnight and thawed before 10 min of sonication in ice-cold water. Liberated DNA from the digested tissue was quantified with PicoGreen DNA kit according to the manufacturer's instructions.

Liberated DNA from the digested tissue was quantified with PicoGreen DNA kit according to the manufacturer's instructions.

subsections.title: 2.8 Analysis of Cartilage Microarchitecture and Proteoglycan Distribution
subsections.content: Cartilage explants were fixed in 10% formalin for 72 h, processed overnight, paraffin embedded, and sectioned at 5 µm. Sections were stained with Safranin-O/Fast Green, and the superficial zone of cartilage sections were imaged and scored blinded by two reviewers (authors ESD and AVM). Histological scoring was performed as previously described to quantify changes in Safranin-O stain's intensity, Fast Green stain's intensity, and the degree of surface irregularities [ 24 ].

Sections were stained with Safranin-O/Fast Green, and the superficial zone of cartilage sections were imaged and scored blinded by two reviewers (authors ESD and AVM). Histological scoring was performed as previously described to quantify changes in Safranin-O stain's intensity, Fast Green stain's intensity, and the degree of surface irregularities [ 24 ].

subsections.title: 2.9 Histochemical and Immunohistochemical Analysis of Synovium
subsections.content: Synovium was fixed in 10% formalin for 48 h before processing and paraffin embedding. Synovium was sectioned at 5 µm, and immunohistochemistry (IHC) was performed to detect synovial macrophages. Synovium sections were stained with primary antibodies diluted 1:2000 for CD86 (M1), CD163 (M2), or CD68 (all macrophages) and counterstained with dilute hematoxylin solution after amplification/detection with the ImPRESS Excel Amplified Polymer Staining Kit. Representative images ( n = 5) were captured using a Keyence BZX 800 microscope at 400x total magnification for each synovium explant and all antibodies used. The number of positively stained macrophages was counted from these images, and the ratio of M1/all macrophages, M2/all macrophages, and M1/M2 macrophages was assessed for each treatment group.

subsections.title: 2.10 Analysis of Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) and Mitochondrial Dysfunction
subsections.content: Synovium and cartilage explants were incubated with MitoSox Red (5 uM) reagent for 30 min at 37°C. After rinsing with PBS, images ( n = 3/synovial explant and n = 3/superficial zone/cartilage explant) were captured with a fluorescent microscope at 400x total magnification. The number of positively stained cells were counted using CellProfiler software.

The number of positively stained cells were counted using CellProfiler software.

subsections.title: 2.11 Inflammation and Protease Arrays on SCM
subsections.content: The presence of cytokines, MMPs, and tissue inhibitors of metalloproteinases (TIMPs) in SCM were quantified using a Human Inflammation Array GS1 and Human MMP Array Q1 according to manufacturer's instructions.
paragraphs: The presence of cytokines, MMPs, and tissue inhibitors of metalloproteinases (TIMPs) in SCM were quantified using a Human Inflammation Array GS1 and Human MMP Array Q1 according to manufacturer's instructions.

subsections.title: 2.12 Statistics
subsections.content: All quantitative data is presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). GraphPad Prism 9 software was used to graph and analyze data for statistical analyses. Ordinary one-way ANOVA with Tukey's multiple comparisons test determined group differences. Statistical differences for percent sGAG loss, inflammatory cytokine and protease array data were determined with a repeated measure mixed effects analysis with multiple comparisons test. Outliers were removed using the ROUT method ( Q = 5%). Groups with missing values were analyzed using the mixed-effects model [ 30 - 32 ].

Outliers were removed using the ROUT method ( Q = 5%). Groups with missing values were analyzed using the mixed-effects model [ 30 - 32 ].

subsections.title: 3.1 Synovium Explant Viability Is Reduced but Metabolic Activity Is Upregulated in OA Only and CSE + OA Conditions
subsections.content: Day 9 Live/Dead images of synovium explants cultured in control, OA only, and CSE + OA conditions qualitatively demonstrated a loss of living (green) cells in treated groups (OA only and CSE + OA) with a more widespread increase in dead (red) cells in the CSE + OA group (Figure 1A ). CSE + OA synovium DNA content was significantly reduced from control ( p = 0.0028) and OA only ( p = 0.0097) synovium (Figure 1B ). subsections.title: 3.2 Mitochondrial Superoxide Production Is Increased in CSE + OA Conditions
subsections.content: Respective images of synovium explants stained with MitoSox Red are shown in Figure 2A , demonstrating differences in the staining intensity and number of cells stained for mitochondrial ROS. Compared to controls, the number of cells positively stained for ROS was significantly higher in the OA only ( p = 0.0018, 95% CI: −15.17 to −4.333) and CSE + OA ( p = 2B ). More importantly, CSE + OA had significantly more cells stained for ROS compared to the OA only group ( p = 0.0172, 95% CI: −12.17 to −1.333).

More importantly, CSE + OA had significantly more cells stained for ROS compared to the OA only group ( p = 0.0172, 95% CI: −12.17 to −1.333).

subsections.title: 3.3 OA Only and CSE + OA Increase the Ratio of M1/M2 Macrophages Versus Controls
subsections.content: To identify relative changes in synovial macrophage polarization, IHC for CD68 (all macrophages), CD86 (M1 phenotype), and CD163 (M2 phenotype) was performed (Figure 3A ). Following culture in control, OA only, and CSE + OA conditions, the ratio of M1/all macrophages was significantly increased in OA only synovium versus controls ( p = 0.0035, 95% CI: −1.292 to −0.2478) and was significantly increased in CSE + OA synovium versus controls ( p = 0.0184, 95% CI: −1.141 to −0.09682) (Figure 3B ). There were no differences in the ratio of M2/all macrophages across any group (Figure 3C ). However, the ratio of M1/M2 macrophages was significantly increased in OA only synovium versus controls ( p = 0.0173, 95% CI: −1.149 to −0.1030) and was significantly increased in CSE + OA synovium versus controls ( p = 0.0245, 95% CI: −1.116 to −0.06995).
paragraphs: To identify relative changes in synovial macrophage polarization, IHC for CD68 (all macrophages), CD86 (M1 phenotype), and CD163 (M2 phenotype) was performed (Figure 3A ). Following culture in control, OA only, and CSE + OA conditions, the ratio of M1/all macrophages was significantly increased in OA only synovium versus controls ( p = 0.0035, 95% CI: −1.292 to −0.2478) and was significantly increased in CSE + OA synovium versus controls ( p = 0.0184, 95% CI: −1.141 to −0.09682) (Figure 3B ). There were no differences in the ratio of M2/all macrophages across any group (Figure 3C ). However, the ratio of M1/M2 macrophages was significantly increased in OA only synovium versus controls ( p = 0.0173, 95% CI: −1.149 to −0.1030) and was significantly increased in CSE + OA synovium versus controls ( p = 0.0245, 95% CI: −1.116 to −0.06995).

subsections.title: 3.4 CSE + OA Treatment Leads to Greater Increases in Proinflammatory Cytokine Production in Synovium Than OA Only Treatment
subsections.content: A proteomic analysis was performed on conditioned media from synovium after 3 and 9 days of exposure to control, OA only, and CSE + OA conditions. On Day 3, IL-1β and TNF-α were significantly elevated in CSE + OA ( p p p p 4A,B ). On Day 9, CSE + OA and OA only synovium explant media contained significantly more IL-1β than controls ( p p p = 0.0187, 95% CI: −10347 to −889.8) on Day 9. On Day 3, CSE + OA- treated synovium produced significantly more IL-8 compared to controls ( p = 0.0029, 95% CI: −1912 to −398.1) and OA only- treated synovium ( p = 0.0127, 95% CI: −1711 to −196.9) (Figure 4C ). MCP-1 was significantly elevated compared to controls in both CSE + OA synovium ( p p = 0.0137, 95% CI: −1217 to −133.3) at Day 3 (Figure 4D ). Compared to controls, CSE + OA- treated synovium also produced significantly more IL-1α ( p = 0.0242, 95% CI: −415.3 to −27.17), IL-6 ( p = 0.0438, 95% CI: −1159 to −14.91), and INF-γ ( p = 0.0030, 95% CI: −902.0 to −186.5) on Day 3 (Figure 4E–G ). Similar trends were observed in the production of anti-inflammatory cytokine IL-13 on Day 3, whereby CSE + OA treated synovium produced significantly more IL-13 ( p = 0.0010, 95% CI: −582.1 to −135.4) compared to controls (Figure 4H ). No differences were observed between any group on Day 9 for IL-8, MCP-1, IL-1α, IL-6, INF-γ, and IL-13. MMP-2 production on Day 3 was higher in CSE + OA-treated synovium explants versus controls ( p = 0.0111, 95% CI: −1871 to −255) and OA only ( p = 0.0059, 95% CI: −1978 to −362.4) synovium explants (Figure 5A ). However, by Day 9, CSE + OA-treated synovium explants produced significantly lower MMP-2 than controls ( p = 0.0116, 95% CI: 248.1 to 1864) and OA only ( p = 0.001, 95% CI: 671.5 to 2287). MMP-3 production on Day 3 was lower in OA only- treated synovium explants versus controls ( p = 0.0446, 95% CI: 17.04 to 1430) (Figure 5B ). MMP-8 production on Day 3 was higher in CSE + OA-treated synovium explants versus OA only ( p = 0.0211, 95% CI: −1082 to −90.18) (Figure 5C ). No other differences in MMP concentrations between any group were noted and no significant differences were noted in TIMP production (Figure 5D,E ). No differences between groups were noted for MMP-9 or MMP-13 production (not shown).

subsections.title: 3.5 CSE + OA SCM-Treated Cartilage Explants Have Reduced Chondrocyte Viability, DNA Content, and Metabolic Activity Compared to Controls
subsections.content: After 3, 6, and 9 days of exposure to control, OA only, and CSE + OA SCM, cartilage explants were evaluated for changes in viability/metabolic activity, loss of ECM, and oxidative stress. Representative fluorescent images of the superficial, middle, and deep zones of cartilage explants following Live/Dead staining (Figure 6A ) demonstrate increases in the number of dead chondrocytes of the OA only and CSE + OA SCM-treated groups as compared to control SCM-treated cartilage. Quantitative assessment of these images demonstrated that there was a significant loss in percent viability in the OA only ( p = 0.0005), and CSE + OA ( p = 0.0004) SCM-treated groups versus controls (Figure 6B ). In support, cartilage explant DNA content was reduced from controls in both OA only ( p = 0.0016, 95% CI: 0.7573 to 2.563), and CSE + OA ( p 6C ). No significant differences were observed in DNA content between CSE + OA and OA only SCM-treated cartilage explants; however, the reduction in DNA content was greater in the CSE + OA group than in the OA-only group, as indicated by a larger effect size. Metabolic activity was significantly reduced from controls in CSE + OA SCM- treated cartilage explants on Day 3 ( p p = 0.0004, 95% CI: 16715 to 40048), and Day 9 ( p = 0.0017, 95% CI: 12199 to 40380) (Figure 6D ). Metabolic activity was only reduced in OA only SCM versus controls on Day 6 ( p = 0.0403, 95% CI: 783.3 to 31135). No differences in metabolic activity between CSE + OA and OA only SCM-treated cartilage explants were observed; however, the reduction in metabolic activity was greater in the CSE + OA group than in the OA-only group, as indicated by a larger effect size.

subsections.title: 3.6 CSE + OA SCM-Treated Cartilage Explants Have Greater Losses in Proteoglycans and Glycosaminoglycans Compared to Controls
subsections.content: Safranin-O/Fast Green staining of cartilage explants demonstrated differences in the distribution and concentration of proteoglycans between groups. Histological imaging on Day 9 indicated a qualitative increase in the intensity of Fast Green stain in CSE + OA and OA only SCM-treated explants versus controls (Figure 7A , top row). Additionally, OA only and CSE + OA images showed a loss of Safranin-O stain versus the controls in the superficial zone (Figure 7A , bottom row). In support, histology scores (Figure 7B ) were significantly reduced in CSE + OA SCM-treated cartilage explants versus controls ( p = 0.004, 95% CI: 0.5823 to 3.168). No differences were observed in sGAG content of cartilage explants between any group (Figure 7C ). However, the percent sGAG loss was higher in the CSE + OA SCM- treated group versus controls on Day 3 ( p = 0.0212, 95% CI: −12.54 to −1.01) and Day 6 ( p = 0.0444, 95% CI: −10.36 to −0.132) (Figure 7D ).

subsections.title: 3.7 More Chondrocytes Stain Positive for Mitochondrial ROS in CSE + OA SCM-Treated Cartilage Explants Versus the OA Only Group
subsections.content: Images of the superficial zone of cartilage explants (Figure 8A ) showed differences in the number, intensity, and depth in which chondrocytes were positively stained for ROS on Day 9. The number of cells positively stained for mitochondrial ROS production was significantly higher in OA only ( p = 0.02, 95% CI: −100.4 to −9.584) and CSE + OA ( p = 0.0002, 95% CI: −156.6 to −65.74) SCM-treated cartilage explants compared to controls (Figure 8B ). More importantly, CSE + OA SCM-treated cartilage explants had significantly more cells stained for ROS than the OA only ( p = 0.0179 95% CI: −101.6 to −10.74) SCM cartilage explants.

sections.title: 4 Discussion
sections.content: Despite the known detrimental impact of cigarette smoking on RA [ 33 - 36 ], little work has been performed to evaluate how cigarette smoking can mediate OA progression by influencing the synovium and impacting cartilage degradation. The synovial membrane acts to maintain joint homeostasis by providing a direct pathway for the exchange of nutrients between blood and articular cartilage, whereby soluble signals cross the synovial barrier into the synovial fluid and diffuse through cartilage ECM to reach chondrocytes [ 37 - 40 ]. As synovium has a direct blood supply and is more sensitive to changes in serum regulators than cartilage, the synovium is likely the primary joint tissue that comes in contact with toxic chemicals from cigarette smoke. This has been demonstrated in an experimental murine model of RA, where high concentrations of cigarette smoking increased synovial immune cell infiltration and synovial hyperplasia, as well as playing a key role in the citrullination of proteins of the synovium [ 41 ]. While the link between tobacco smoking and the initiation and perpetuation of inflammation in the synovial membrane of patients with RA has been reviewed, its effects of OA synovium have yet to be studied [ 42 ]. As such, the present study investigated the effects of CSE and OA-like inflammation on synovial tissue and the influence this complex milieu has on cartilage explants. As part of the complexity and physiological relevance of this model, co-stimulation with IL-1β and TNF-α was used to induce an inflammatory reaction similar to that of naturally occurring OA (OA-like inflammation) and is supported by prior work in both chondrocyte and tissue explant models [ 43 , 44 ]. The primary findings from this work were that CSE + OA synovium was similar to OA only- treated synovium with respect to changes in cell viability, metabolic activity, and the proportion of M1/M2 macrophages. However, CSE + OA-treated synovium produced significantly more IL-8, MMP-2, and MMP-8 on Day 3 and had significantly more cells stained positive for mitochondrial ROS on Day 9 compared to OA only- treated synovium. Secondarily, cartilage explant treatment with CSE and OA-like inflammatory SCM resulted in more pronounced decreases in metabolic activity and ECM integrity relative to controls compared with OA-only SCM. Further, cartilage explants cultured under CSE + OA SCM had significantly more cells stained positive for ROS compared to those cultured in OA only SCM. Altogether, our results suggest that CSE has the potential to alter the synovial secretome under inflammatory conditions, resulting in increased joint tissue damage and oxidative stress.

subsections.title: 4.1 Effect of CSE + OA on Synovium
subsections.content: Inflammatory microenvironments have been linked to metabolic dysregulation in cells of the synovium and chondrocytes, playing a pivotal role in cartilage degeneration and OA progression [ 40 , 45 ]. While dysregulation of the synovium has been implicated in the pathogenesis of OA, it has been hypothesized that synovitis occurs before visible osteoarthritic changes occur in cartilage and subchondral bone, suggesting its initiating role in joint tissue destruction [ 38 , 46 - 51 ]. Specifically, activated fibroblast-like synoviocytes (FLS) in OA synovium secrete cytokines, growth factors, MMPs, and TIMPs, which contribute to macrophage activation and stimulate catabolic pathways in chondrocytes [ 38 , 52 ]. In this work, synovium under CSE + OA and OA only conditions demonstrated increased metabolic activity compared to controls. This was unexpected and seemingly conflicts with our viability results, which demonstrated a loss of living/increase in dead cells present in OA only and CSE + OA treated synovium. However, it has been shown that RA-affected synovial cells in adverse environments switch their metabolism from a resting regulatory state to a highly metabolically active state [ 53 ]. This alters regulatory pathways and results in the accumulation of signaling molecules that can further exacerbate the inflammatory response [ 54 , 55 ]. As such, increased synovium metabolic activity in this work is indicative of an inflammatory environment [ 51 , 56 ]. While, in this case, our data suggests that CSE does not increase synovium metabolic activity more so than OA-like inflammation alone, it does provide insight into the direct response of synovial tissue to these conditions, which may have downstream effects on cartilage homeostasis. Elevated production of ROS is another feature of the OA-inflamed microenvironment, where excessive amounts of ROS lead to modifications in intracellular signaling, cartilage metabolism, and ECM degradation, synovial inflammation, and dysfunction of the subchondral bone [ 57 - 60 ]. Cigarette smoke is known to be both a source and an inducer of cellular oxidative stress, where cigarette smoking has been shown to contribute to mitochondrial-DNA damage (mtDNA), which then results in ROS production [ 61 - 63 ]. The present work found, for the first time, that the number of cells in synovium staining positive for mitochondrial ROS was significantly higher in the CSE + OA -treated synovium versus the OA only- treated synovium. This is, in part, supported by previous in vivo work by Fernandez-Torres et al., who demonstrated that the levels of ROS found in human OA synovial fluid of current smokers were higher than in former or non-smokers [ 21 ]. This data suggests that CSE and/or smoking conditions have a cumulative impact on ROS overproduction in the joint with the potential to accelerate OA pathogenesis. Furthermore, our results indicate that mitochondrial damage may be involved in the pathway underpinning these changes. Imbalanced M1/M2 ratios of macrophage phenotypes have been significantly related to OA severity and have also been reported in the airways of smokers [ 64 ]. M1 macrophages accumulate more in the synovial membrane of experimental OA, exacerbating the progression of the disease by producing high levels of proinflammatory cytokines, such as IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α and IFNγ [ 65 , 66 ]. M2 macrophages are involved in tissue repair, suppressing the immune response, inducing cartilage synthesis and inhibiting chondrocyte apoptosis via the production of IL-10 and transforming growth factor (TGF)-β [ 65 , 66 ]. In this work, the ratio of M1/M2 macrophages was equally elevated in CSE + OA and OA only- treated synovium, suggesting an inflammatory response of the synovium, but did not support our hypothesis and demonstrates that in this model CSE has no cumulative effect on synovium macrophage polarization under OA-like conditions. This may have been affected by the qualitative approach rather than quantitative or may be because inflammatory cell infiltration cannot occur in this explant model and thus will need to be studied further in vivo. However, cytokine analysis of SCM indicated that CSE can augment the proinflammatory secretory profile of synovium, characteristic of M1 macrophage polarization, more so than OA only conditions. This is evident in our results demonstrating that CSE + OA substantially increased the production of MCP-1, IL-1α, IL-6, and INF-γ versus controls, whereas OA only- treated synovium did not. Further, chemokine production of IL-8, and production of MMP-2 and MMP-8 were significantly elevated in CSE + OA synovium versus OA only. Cigarette smoking has a history of increasing IL-8 chemokine production in various types of cells [ 67 - 71 ]. Chemokines, in response to cytokines, attract additional inflammatory cells to the joint in vivo, exacerbating the inflammatory state and promoting disease progression [ 6 , 72 , 73 ]. IL-8 is also one of the most detected chemokines released by neutrophils and in active synovial inflammation [ 74 ]. Cigarette smoking has also shown to increase MMP-2 production in fibroblasts and to effect neutrophil function, a major source of MMP-8, both of which are heavily involved in the matrix destructive processes associated with OA [ 75 - 77 ]. While very few investigations into how smoking alters OA synovium have been undertaken, these results support previous work investigating the effects of smoking on RA, where different cigarette smoke preparations induce proinflammatory and matrix-destructive responses, including increased production/expression of interleukin IL-1α, IL-1β, IL-6, and IL-8 in human FLS [ 34 ], as well as additional work demonstrating cigarette smoking increases MMPs in RA synovial tissue and FLS [ 78 ]. However, in the work that compares the response of RA- to OA-patient derived synoviocytes and synovial tissue, exposure to cigarette smoke chemicals enhanced RA inflammatory processes more than OA [ 33 , 79 ]. This may be due to smoking increasing the formation of citrullinated proteins, which can lead to autoimmunity to citrullinated proteins and subsequently the development of RA [ 80 - 82 ]. In addition, cytokine and MMP production by synovial tissue explants generally decreases over time. At Day 3, both OA only and CSE + OA synovium exhibited robust increases in pro-inflammatory cytokines, chemokines, and select MMPs, which is consistent with heightened M1 macrophage polarization and activation. However, by Day 9, many of these elevations were diminished despite persistently increased mitochondrial ROS and an altered M1/M2 ratio. Thus, this temporal decline in cytokine and MMP production from Day 3 to Day 9 suggests a shift from an early acute inflammatory response to a later state of oxidative stress–induced cellular dysfunction. The reduction in secretory output also coincided with reduced DNA content and increased cell death, particularly in the CSE + OA group. Although metabolic activity remained elevated, this likely reflects a stress-associated hypermetabolic state in a reduced population of surviving cells rather than preserved functional output. Together, these findings support a model in which CSE exposure amplifies early inflammatory activation in osteoarthritic synovium but drives a transition toward oxidative damage, cellular exhaustion, and diminished matrix-remodeling capacity over time.

subsections.title: 4.2 Effect of CSE + OA SCM on Cartilage
subsections.content: The communication of synovial cells (fibroblasts, immune cells, and macrophages) with chondrocytes is essential for maintaining joint homeostasis [ 83 ]. Activated synovial tissue macrophages (STMs) stimulated to undergo M1 polarization secrete proinflammatory mediators that affect chondrocytes, promoting the degradation of ECM components [ 38 , 52 ]. In response to inflammatory mediators, chondrocytes also produce abnormal levels of ROS through NADPH oxidase, which activates several signaling pathways, further contributing to chondrocyte apoptosis, cytokine production, and ECM breakdown [ 84 ]. In this work, mitochondrial ROS was significantly increased in CSE + OA SCM versus OA only SCM-treated explants. This may be due to the increased levels of inflammatory cytokines present in our system or the innate potential for cigarette smoke to induce oxidative stress. Nonetheless, this data supports previous in vitro work by our lab and work by Chen et al., who investigated the effects of CSE on chondrocytes in monolayer, both demonstrating the ability of CSE to contribute to ROS production in chondrocytes [ 24 ]. Despite the increased production of IL-8, MMP-2, and MMP-8, the loss of ECM components and matrix integrity in cartilage explants in CSE + OA SCM was not significantly increased versus OA only SCM conditions. Though, this may be due to the short-term response, whereby chemokine and protease differences were only observed on Day 3 and not Day 9, and/or the increase in anti-inflammatory mediators and their chondroprotective properties. However, it is important to note that analysis of cartilage ECM indicated that CSE may still have a cumulative effect on OA-like inflammation. Like the cytokine analysis, this is evident in our results, demonstrating that CSE + OA SCM significantly increased sGAG loss versus controls, whereas OA only SCM did not. This indicates that CSE may accelerate the destruction of articular cartilage under inflammatory conditions. Though there were no significant differences in GAG loss between the control an OA only SCM groups, this finding was consistent with our previous cartilage only model [ 20 ].
paragraphs: The communication of synovial cells (fibroblasts, immune cells, and macrophages) with chondrocytes is essential for maintaining joint homeostasis [ 83 ]. Activated synovial tissue macrophages (STMs) stimulated to undergo M1 polarization secrete proinflammatory mediators that affect chondrocytes, promoting the degradation of ECM components [ 38 , 52 ]. In response to inflammatory mediators, chondrocytes also produce abnormal levels of ROS through NADPH oxidase, which activates several signaling pathways, further contributing to chondrocyte apoptosis, cytokine production, and ECM breakdown [ 84 ]. In this work, mitochondrial ROS was significantly increased in CSE + OA SCM versus OA only SCM-treated explants. This may be due to the increased levels of inflammatory cytokines present in our system or the innate potential for cigarette smoke to induce oxidative stress. Nonetheless, this data supports previous in vitro work by our lab and work by Chen et al., who investigated the effects of CSE on chondrocytes in monolayer, both demonstrating the ability of CSE to contribute to ROS production in chondrocytes [ 24 ]. Despite the increased production of IL-8, MMP-2, and MMP-8, the loss of ECM components and matrix integrity in cartilage explants in CSE + OA SCM was not significantly increased versus OA only SCM conditions. Though, this may be due to the short-term response, whereby chemokine and protease differences were only observed on Day 3 and not Day 9, and/or the increase in anti-inflammatory mediators and their chondroprotective properties. However, it is important to note that analysis of cartilage ECM indicated that CSE may still have a cumulative effect on OA-like inflammation. Like the cytokine analysis, this is evident in our results, demonstrating that CSE + OA SCM significantly increased sGAG loss versus controls, whereas OA only SCM did not. This indicates that CSE may accelerate the destruction of articular cartilage under inflammatory conditions. Though there were no significant differences in GAG loss between the control an OA only SCM groups, this finding was consistent with our previous cartilage only model [ 20 ].

subsections.title: 4.3 Significance, Limitations, and Future Work
subsections.content: Together, the increase in the number of cells positive for ROS and the overproduction of both pro- and anti-inflammatory mediators indicate a complex response of cells of the synovium to CSE. Though synovial inflammation is less pronounced in OA compared to RA, it has been proven to have a pathogenic role in increasing both pain and disease progression [ 51 ]. Understanding the mechanisms behind cigarette smoke-mediated changes in synovial inflammation may help in designing treatments to modulate macrophage activity, reduce inflammation, and promote joint homeostasis. This is important as cigarette smoking has been associated with more musculoskeletal pain, and after joint replacement surgery, tobacco smokers have been found to have poor pain control compared to nonsmokers, requiring significantly greater doses of narcotic pain medications [ 17 , 21 , 85 ]. In support of this, cigarette smoking is known to impact the absorption, distribution, metabolism, and elimination of certain drugs, reducing the therapeutic efficacy and drug survival of anti-TNF therapies for the treatment of RA [ 86 ]. As such, studying the effects of cigarette smoking on OA utilizing relevant joint system models may be able to assist in the development of more effective pain-relieving and/or disease-modifying therapeutics for OA patients with a physiological dependency on cigarette smoking. Limitations of this study arise from using an indirect co-culture model. Specifically, the reciprocal paracrine signaling from cartilage on synovium is absent, which has been shown to play a major role in the pathogenesis of OA [ 4 , 87 ]. This limitation could be resolved using a direct co-culture model or culturing synovium and cartilage in the same well. However, this would have prevented us from evaluating the effects of CSE on OA-like inflammation in synovium alone. Additionally, once inflammatory cytokines are introduced to stimulate synovial tissue, it is not feasible to distinguish exogenously added cytokines from those subsequently produced and secreted by synovial cells, and any removal strategy would non-selectively eliminate both pools. The same concept applies to the addition of CSE. However, direct carryover effects are expected to be minimal, as IL-1β, TNF-α, and many bioactive components of CSE exhibit short extracellular half-lives and decay rapidly during incubation. Thus, while the indirect co-culture model remains appropriate for assessing synovium mediated effects on cartilage, the potential contribution of residual inflammatory stimuli should be considered when interpreting these findings. Further, synovial tissue exhibits inherent regional and phenotypic heterogeneity, including the presence of adipose-rich synovial subtypes [ 37 ]. Although synovium was carefully isolated from the lateral joint compartment to avoid infrapatellar fat pad tissue, we cannot fully exclude the possibility that adipose-associated characteristics contributed to variability in synovial responses. As this is also a closed system, macrophage infiltration is not possible, so only changes in phenotypes of the STMs that were already present upon isolation can be evaluated. To address this, further work will need to employ the use of an animal model to assess the effects of cigarette smoking on inflammatory cell infiltration of the synovium and other characteristic features of the OA synovium, particularly as IL-8 plays a role in inflammatory cell homing and infiltration to the synovium [ 74 , 88 ]. Lastly, tissue from skeletally immature animals was utilized in this work, which may have impacted our results as younger, healthy tissue (in particular chondrocytes) has differing cellular responses to inflammation than older, or damaged tissue [ 89 , 90 ].

sections.title: 5 Conclusion
sections.content: CSE may accelerate OA progression by altering the inflammatory secretory profile of synovium and contributing to increased oxidative stress and the pathological crosstalk between synovium and cartilage.
paragraphs: CSE may accelerate OA progression by altering the inflammatory secretory profile of synovium and contributing to increased oxidative stress and the pathological crosstalk between synovium and cartilage.
